issues.title: Add Security sub-section of DOS risks
issues.author: identitymonk
issues.body:
I think there are two DOS risks on the authorization endpoint (and other endpoints that could accept CIMD as parameter) that might worth spelling it out in the security section of this specification:

- I could force through multiple authorization requests to an AS with CIMD hosted at a slow responding endpoint mobilizing resources at the AS up to a DOS situation

** Proposed text **
```
Client ID Metadata Document Fetch DOS

Authorization servers fetching client metadata documents SHOULD implement appropriate rate limiting and timeout mechanisms
when retrieving Client ID Metadata Documents. A malicious actor could attempt to cause a denial of service by initiating multiple
authorization requests using a client_id that points to an intentionally slow-responding endpoint, potentially exhausting authorization server resources.

Authorization servers SHOULD implement measures from this non exhaustive list:
- implement standard anonymous and bad reputation IP filtering, as well as DNS name filtering
- Implement strict timeouts when fetching metadata documents
- Rate limit requests per client_id URL and/or requesting IP
- Consider caching valid responses as specified in Section 4.4
- Terminate requests that exceed reasonable response times
```

- I could force through multiple authorization requests to an AS to fetch what could be CIMD at 3rd party, mobilizing resources at this 3rd party up to a DOS situation

** Proposed text **
```
Third-Party Endpoint DOS Protection

Authorization servers MUST take precautions to prevent their metadata document fetching mechanism from being used as an
amplification vector in DOS attacks against third-party endpoints. A malicious actor could attempt to use the authorization
server's metadata fetching capabilities to direct high volumes of requests to an arbitrary third-party target.

Authorization servers SHOULD implement measures from this non exhaustive list:
- implement standard anonymous and bad reputation IP filtering
- Implement per-endpoint rate limiting for metadata document fetches
- Monitor for patterns of repeated requests to the same endpoints
- Consider maintaining allow-lists and deny-lists of trusted domains for metadata documents
- Implement circuit breakers to prevent cascading failures
```

comments.author: ThisIsMissEm
comments.body:
I think many of these mitigations are just general OAuth security recommendations?

Yes, we could do with a security consideration section on timeouts, but those are very deployment specific as to what makes sense for your deployment. The most we could probably say is that as you're making an outbound HTTP request, to ensure that you set appropriate timeouts around making that request.

As for the DOS aspect, a lot of these can be mitigated by deferring the fetching of the Client ID Metadata Document until after the user is logged in, i.e., not fetching the document the moment you receive the authorization request or PAR.

comments.author: identitymonk
comments.body:
Yes and no. Yes, those are standard web endpoint mitigations but fetching "per authorization request" is really a stance that was not approach until now in OAuth. 

I like"deferring the fetching of the Client ID Metadata Document until after the user is logged in" as a MUST, while the rest might remain an Informative Guidance.

comments.author: ThisIsMissEm
comments.body:
> I like"deferring the fetching of the Client ID Metadata Document until after the user is logged in" as a MUST, while the rest might remain an Informative Guidance.

This does change the error handling situation here, for instance, a poorly formed Client ID Metadata Document then likely gives the user a dead-end in the authorization flow, especially with PARs.

I know Aaron has a separate issue with a recommendation to only fetch documents after login.

> This does change the error handling situation here, for instance, a poorly formed Client ID Metadata Document then likely gives the user a dead-end in the authorization flow, especially with PARs.

Agreed but it gates abuse attempts through a valid user authentication. Having a user succeeding in authentication but with invalid CIMD is another type of signal that the user might be compromised.

issues.title: Add security consideration around boundary of client identity
issues.author: aaronpk
issues.body:
An AS can decide what it considers the security boundary client identity. For example, an AS can decide that the domain name is the security boundary of the client, and any path variations on the domain are considered the "same client" for purposes like auto-approving previously authorized OAuth grants.

e.g. `https://example.com/client/v1.json` `https://example.com/client/v2.json` could be considered different versions of the same client.

The AS may want to take various factors into account, like only considering the domain the boundary for trusted domains, to avoid domains commonly used for hosting services like azure that are shared across multiple customers.

comments.author: ThisIsMissEm
comments.body: I think if two clients are the "same" we should create a relationship between them explicitly.

comments.author: aaronpk
comments.body:
Actually this is what `software_id` in the client registration vocabulary means. So maybe we could use that:

```
{
  "client_id": "https://example.com/client/v1.json",
  "software_id": "https://example.com/client.json"
}
```

There would need to be a way to link back from `https://example.com/client.json` to `https://example.com/client/v1.json` otherwise it doesn't solve the problem of the overlap on shared domains.

Any new actual mechanism should probably be done as a separate extension. But I thought it would be worth pointing out non-normatively in security considerations that an AS might want to have its own policies around domain trust, similar to other things mentioned about domain trust.

fwiw this came up from a discussion with someone who was hoping to be able to publish updated metadata like the redirect URI but keep the client ID value the same to avoid an AS revoking tokens and prior user consent.

comments.author: ThisIsMissEm
comments.body: I think there's probably some fancy bidirectional linking that can be done here, but it may need an additional metadata field (or a meta-client document)

comments.author: ThisIsMissEm
comments.body: This is very much related to #32

comments.author: max-stytch
comments.body:
I'm a fan of opt-in additional metadata fields within the CIMD over a new meta-client document. Or a new `software_id` document - `software_id` is also currently defined as an opaque string, not a URL — so there's no way to dereference it and link back to the clients that claim it. 

What if we built the bidirectionality directly into CIMD with a root/instance model? (naming... to be improved on?)

Root document — the canonical client identity. A complete CIMD document that additionally declares URL patterns for deployments it considers part of the same logical client:
```
  {
    "client_id": "https://example.com/client.json",
    "client_id_type": "root",
    "client_instance_patterns": [
      "https://example.com/client/v*.json"
    ],
    "client_name": "My App",
    "redirect_uris": ["https://example.com/callback"],
    "token_endpoint_auth_method": "none"
  }
```

Instance document — a complete, fully standalone CIMD document that additionally declares a relationship to a root:
```
  {
    "client_id": "https://example.com/client/v2.json",
    "client_id_type": "instance",
    "client_root": "https://example.com/client.json",
    "client_name": "My App",
    "redirect_uris": ["https://example.com/callback2"],
    "token_endpoint_auth_method": "none"
  }
```
An instance document is a fully valid CIMD document on its own. The `client_id_type` and `client_root` fields are additional metadata. An AS that doesn't understand root/instance ignores those fields and processes the document normally — it just sees a regular client.

An AS that does support the extension can:
1. Fetch the instance, note the `client_root`
2. Fetch the root, confirm the instance's `client_id` matches a pattern in `client_instance_patterns`
3. Treat the instance as the same logical client as the root for purposes of consent, grants, and admin allowlisting

Both sides must agree — the instance claims a root, and the root's pattern list confirms the claim. The AS may want to verify that some security-critical fields are consistent between the two - the same way the AS needs to handle metadata changing in `6.3.  Changes in Client Metadata` today.

comments.author: max-stytch
comments.body:
Dialing back the solutioning a tad from my last comment:
- I agree with @ThisIsMissEm's comment from October that the trust boundaries should be explicit. If trust boundaries are implicit and defined by the AS, then the same client (or... set of CIMD documents) can be parsed and treated differently by different ASs and we will get interoperability issues.
- I think that the existing CIMD document is the best place to define the trust boundary, instead of creating a new meta-document or software-statement structure

comments.author: itsvs
comments.body:
I think we could establish links between clients without the need for an additional meta-client, `software_id`, or root client document. #67 suggests adding guidance around how clients can identify versions of themselves, and we could build on that:

<details>

<summary> (example collapsed for space) </summary>

```diff
{
    "client_id": "example.com/client/prod-v1.0.json",
    "software_id": "example.com/client/prod",
    "software_version": "v1.0",
+   "next_software_version": "v1.1"
}

{
    "client_id": "example.com/client/prod-v1.1.json",
    "software_id": "example.com/client/prod",
    "software_version": "v1.1",
+   "prev_software_version": "v1.0"
+   "next_software_version": "v1.2"
}

{
    "client_id": "example.com/client/prod-v1.2.json",
    "software_id": "example.com/client/prod",
    "software_version": "v1.2",
+   "prev_software_version": "v1.1"
}
```

</details>

This allows for a couple of things:
- The client can use `software_id` to indicate a _minimum_ trust boundary, i.e. "even if an AS treats different versions as part of the same client, it MUST NOT treat different `software_id`s the same."
  - This also allows for multiple independent client version chains, e.g. the client can have another `software_id: example.com/client/dev` that has different capabilities and perhaps localhost redirects.
- The client can present a trust chain within that boundary as a suggestion to the AS, i.e. "`v1.2` is just a new version of the same software."
- The AS can use all of that information to decide if it wants to:
  - Treat different `client_id`s as different clients
  - Treat different `software_id`s as different clients
  - (some third option)

> keep the client ID value the same to avoid an AS revoking tokens and prior user consent

At the end of the day, I think this is entirely up to the AS, but I do agree that the client should be able to indicate what should happen.

@ThisIsMissEm, this slightly modifies my thoughts in #67 -- I'll add a comment there indicating that :)

issues.title: Changes in Client Keys can happen for non-nefarious reasons
issues.author: bc-pi
issues.body:
The text in https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-oauth-client-id-metadata-document-00#name-changes-in-client-keys suggests that a change in the content of the jwks_uri might indicate a condition that would need some kind of remediation or proactive disruption of something. 

However the key rotation dance in OIDC at https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0.html#RotateSigKeys and https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0.html#RotateEncKeys has the possibility of changes in the content of the jwks_uri as a normal course of events. And while that is in OIDC and this isn't OIDC, the pattern has permeated into the OAuth sphere. I think that if some of the actions from https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-oauth-client-id-metadata-document-00#name-changes-in-client-keys were taken, it would be detrimental to interop. 

Can this draft tone down the suggestions in Changes in Client Keys?

comments.author: bc-pi
comments.body: Recently came across some prospective design work on supporting CIMD that had an open question about what doing this would mean and what the implications might be. Which was just a reminder to me that the draft would do well to tone down the suggestions in Changes in Client Keys.

comments.author: aaronpk
comments.body: please see the changes in the latest version. I am not sure about removing the mention of it entirely, so instead I moved this under "metadata changes" and added a mention that key changes can also be normal. I'm open to further suggestions though.

comments.author: bc-pi
comments.body: Thanks Aaron, perhaps we can discuss more at 125?  That change is moving in the right direction I think but IMHO could use more in that direction and feels maybe a bit self contradictory now.

issues.title: Align `abbrev` with CIMD as is commonly used
issues.author: ThisIsMissEm
issues.body:
Currently the `abbrev` in the draft's metadata is `"Client ID Document"`, we should probably align this to be `CIMD`, as that's what we've started using everywhere (e.g., https://cimd.dev ). The existing abbrev also missing the "Metadata" which is kind of important.

I'm not sure we can change this now, but if we can, it'd be good to.

comments.author: aaronpk
comments.body: I don't think it's a problem to change it

issues.title: Add a mechanism for client-triggered early metadata retrieval
issues.author: TakahikoKawasaki
issues.body:
While authorization servers can calculate the lifetime of client metadata using the methods described in [RFC 9111 HTTP Caching](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111.html) and continue to reuse the retrieved metadata until the calculated expiration time is reached, client applications may sometimes want authorization servers to re-fetch the metadata before that expiration time.

Could we introduce a mechanism that allows client applications to trigger such early metadata retrieval? For example, what about introducing a new request parameter like `cimd_max_age`, similar in concept to the `max_age` parameter defined in [OpenID Connect Core 1.0](https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0.html)?

This issue is related to PR #29 "Improve documentation for development usage and expiration of clients", I think.

comments.author: panva
comments.body: Just prescribing to use a "max-age" HTTP Response Header would probably be cleaner than introducing a new server metadata.

comments.author: TakahikoKawasaki
comments.body:
@panva Thank you for your comment.

This is not a proposal to introduce new server metadata, but rather a new runtime request parameter.

I implemented CIMD with a caching mechanism that leverages existing HTTP semantics, including `max-age`. However, I still believe that having a mechanism to trigger earlier metadata retrieval would be beneficial.

[OpenID Federation](https://openid.net/specs/openid-federation-1_0.html) defines the `trust_chain` request parameter. When included in an authorization request, it instructs the authorization server to update the client metadata using the provided trust chain, even if the cached trust chain has not yet expired. I hope that CIMD can also offer a similar mechanism to trigger early metadata retrieval, analogous to the `trust_chain` request parameter.

comments.author: panva
comments.body:
Ah, sorry, then let me rephrase.

Just prescribing to use a "max-age" HTTP Response Header from a CIMD response would be cleaner than introducing a new request parameter that could actually be at odds with an existing max-age on the response and then we'd have to prescribe which of the max ages to use.

A parameter like so could also be abused to force AS cache invalidation which could cause to a whole sorts of otherwise avoidable issues. If the only thing the server trusts to control its cache ttl is a max-age response header than that's one less moving piece.

comments.author: aaronpk
comments.body:
On reading the title, I thought you were going to suggest a totally new endpoint the client could use to request updated metadata fetching, not during authorization. 

I agree with Filip's concerns about adding a parameter in the authorize request. Ultimately an AS would decide its own rate limit of the max age parameter anyway to avoid too many requests from clients that always set it to 0.

comments.author: TakahikoKawasaki
comments.body:
I'm not suggesting a new endpoint, which would be heavyweight. 😅

Perhaps the name `cimd_max_age` wasn't ideal. What I really wanted is a mechanism that forces the authorization server to re-fetch client metadata. (Like "super reload" of web browsers)

During client application development, developers may not be able to properly configure the web server that publishes the client metadata—either due to lack of knowledge or insufficient permissions. In such cases (including simple mistakes like forgetting to set `max-age` or accidentally setting it too long), even if the client metadata is updated frequently during development, the authorization server will continue using the cached metadata, which slows down the development process. I wanted a way to address this situation.

comments.author: panva
comments.body: That's a very coincidental problem to have to deal with. As a developer i might as well just randomize the URLs I use during development then can't I?

comments.author: aaronpk
comments.body: My point is even if you want to force the AS to reload the metadata, the AS might always have a policy that prevents reloading at some interval. At most this new parameter would be a suggestion, and we already have a mechanism to suggest lifetime of the metadata in the HTTP header.

issues.title: Clarify jwks vs jwks_uri support
issues.author: yissellokta
issues.body:
The Problem
Section 4.1 pulls in jwks via the DCR registry, but Section 6.3 only discusses rotation/updates for jwks_uri. This leaves the expected behavior for embedded jwks unclear.

The Proposal
I suggest we only support jwks_uri for CIMD and disallow embedded jwks.

Reasons:
- Reduced Complexity: For Dynamic CIMD, the Authorization Server (AS) shouldn't have to re-parse the entire metadata document just to find a key change.
- Clean Invalidation: Invalidation is much cleaner when the cryptographic identity is hosted at a separate jwks_uri rather than inside the metadata.
- Existing Patterns: Current implementations already focus on jwks_uri for dynamic rotation.

**Question**
Does the group see a hard requirement for keeping jwks embedded, or can we move to a jwks_uri only model to simplify implementation?

comments.author: aaronpk
comments.body: I would be in favor of this suggestion to remove `jwks` as an option from the metadata for the reasons described here.

comments.author: ThisIsMissEm
comments.body:
I am aware of in the wild usage of jwks in CIMDs, however, it's not something I'd encourage; using a separate document for JWKS is probably best practice here anyway.

I'd be in favour of deprecation of jwks in CIMDs, and a technical note for why that is the way it is.

comments.author: ThisIsMissEm
comments.body: @aaronpk I think we'd have to make `jwks` embedded in a CIMD a SHOULD NOT instead of a MUST NOT, due to the in-the-wild usage currently present. Happy to do up a small pull request for this, that says it's a SHOULD NOT and then explains why that's the case, and why you should use `jwks_uri` instead.

comments.author: btiernay
comments.body:
> I am aware of in the wild usage of jwks in CIMD

@ThisIsMissEm curious, do you have any public examples you can share?

comments.author: ThisIsMissEm
comments.body:
@btiernay smokesignal.events uses it: https://smokesignal.events/oauth-client-metadata.json

I'm sure I've seen other such examples too.

comments.author: panva
comments.body:
I tried approaching this question from many different angles and came to the conclusion that there's little if any reason for a SHOULD NOT/MUST NOT here.

A regular client key rotation during which the client first publishes its new keys but doesn't use them can happen in both jwks and jwks_uri. In both cases the client controls the max-age of the particular http response and in doing so the time it knows to wait before starting to use a new key.

The only reason to push for jwks_uri is the 5kb recommendation on CIMD document size and public key sizes, but who's to say that limit doesn't exist on client's jwks_uri...

I think each client should be able to choose whichever scheme works for its deployment and looking at an AS implementation myself, I have no reason to prefer one over the other. Rotation via jwks_uri is an established pattern. Rotation via natural expiry and regular re-fetching of CIMD document comes out of the box with CIMD

comments.author: aaronpk
comments.body: My main interest in this is reducing optionality to make things simpler for implementers.

issues.title: Recommed well-known authorization metadata indicating CIMD support
issues.author: meghnadubey
issues.body: This spec should recommend `.well-known/oauth-authorization-server` metadata that will indicate that the server supports CIMD for clients. Something like a `client_methods_supported` or `client_identification_supported`, can indicate to a client whether the server will support CIMD or if the client has to register at the server first.

comments.author: ThisIsMissEm
comments.body: We have this: https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-ietf-oauth-client-id-metadata-document-00.html#name-authorization-server-metada

issues.title: Concerns on Client Identifier allowing Query Strings
issues.author: MichaelFraser99
issues.body:
The draft's text includes the following stipulation:

```
Client identifier URLs SHOULD NOT include a query string component
```

Given query strings are inherently unordered, this leads to the scenario where client identifier comparisons using strict string comparison may fail for two URLs that are still semantically the same. As such, I believe we should explicitly ban the use of them to reduce ambiguity.

Additionally, it's worth noting that OpenID Federation 1.0 (referenced by the document as another example of Client Identifiers being URLs) explicitly bans Query parameters in this location so aligning for interoperability may also be valuable.

issues.title: Consider including explicit guidance for deployments in ecosystems with optionality
issues.author: MichaelFraser99
issues.body:
[OpenID Relying Party Metadata Choices](https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-rp-metadata-choices-1_0.html) introduces a set of metadata for a scenario where an RP needs to advertise support for a set of metadata that itself is a single-value claim. Such metadata is intended to be used where an RP _may_ support multiple different options for a given claim such as `token_endpoint_auth_method` that a given ecosystem or domain may support.

While nothing in this document explicitly prohibits this, an implementation consideration would be valuable here. 

Perhaps some text akin to:

```markdown
In a deployment where a given Client may be expected to interact 
with servers that support multiple different options for single-value 
claims, the claims registered in the [OpenID Relying Party Metadata Choices](https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-rp-metadata-choices-1_0.html) 
document SHOULD be used.
```

issues.title: Define AS behavior when CIMD contains unsupported metadata values
issues.author: panva
issues.body:
CIMD Section 4.1 client metadata values are those from the DCR Metadata registry but doesn't say what an AS should do when the document contains values the AS doesn't support - for instance a `grant_types` entry like `urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code` on an AS that doesn't offer DAG.

Both RFC 7591 and OpenID Connect DCR explicitly permit the AS to either reject or substitute/filter unsupported metadata. RFC 7591 says the AS "MAY reject or replace any of the client's requested metadata values [...] and substitute them with suitable values" and that the client can then "check the values in the response to determine if the registration is sufficient for use."

That last part is key - DCR has a negotiation step. The client sends requested metadata, gets back what the AS actually registered, and decides whether it can work with that. Both behaviors (reject or filter) are fine because the client gets feedback.

CIMD doesn't have this (by design). The client publishes one static document for all ASs. There's no response for the client to inspect. Without guidance in the spec, AS implementers may default to the stricter DCR behavior (reject), either by choice or unknowingly by reusing the DCR client metadata validations, which is at odds with clients having a single general purpose static document.

Example: VS Code publishes `https://vscode.dev/oauth/client-metadata.json` with `grant_types` including `authorization_code`, `refresh_token`, and `urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code`. An AS that supports CIMD but not DAG may reject the whole thing, even though `authorization_code` would work fine.

The spec could say that when processing a CIMD, the AS should filter array-valued fields like `grant_types` and `response_types` to the intersection of what the client declares and what the AS supports, and must reject only when that intersection is empty. This is consistent with what DCR already allows - it just makes filtering the expected default for CIMD, which makes sense given the lack of a negotiation step.

But the remaining issue is the potential lack of upfront knowledge. Especially for client metadata related to AS metadata that is defined as optional such as grant_types_supported. Note that I now realize grant_types_supported does have a default specified when omitted.

So I wonder if, combined with the prescribed non-eager reject behaviour, select AS metadata without a default should in addition be made REQUIRED when used in combination with CIMD so that the client knows (or at least can infer) what the resulting metadata likely is that the AS will use. (note I didn't give much thought into scouring each metadata definition for a list just yet)

Refs: https://github.com/panva/node-oidc-provider/issues/1398

issues.title: Clients shouldn't rely on CIMD for their own configuration
issues.author: aaronpk
issues.body:
https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/oauth/VZnfgpw1bLbgyQZ6HonqYb3O2TE/

> While the Client ID Metadata Document is intended to enable authorization servers to obtain client metadata, it occurred to me that client developers might be tempted to use their own CIMD as local configuration.

> I think it would be worthwhile to add a paragraph to the Security Considerations section to discourage that use. A client that relies on the redirect_uris from its own CIMD could cause authorization servers to send an authorization code to an attacker-controlled endpoint if the CIMD is ever compromised, even if the authorization server performs exact redirect URI matching. The use of PKCE may reduce the impact of authorization code disclosure, but it does not eliminate the need to protect redirect handling and related metadata.

comments.author: ThisIsMissEm
comments.body:
I think this is "fine" in that if your CIMD is compromised, then your server entirely is compromised, so all bets are off.

Hardcoding your `redirect_uri` to match one in the CIMD is a good practice, which we should encourage, especially for CIMDs not hosted by the application server. (e.g., cimd-service or split domain CIMDs)

comments.author: ThisIsMissEm
comments.body: However, a client _may_ fetch a remote CIMD document, to verify the values passed to the `authorize()` call to kick off the OAuth flow are indeed actually valid parameters according to the CIMD.

issues.title: Are SSRF checks when AS is running on loopback needed for URLs *within* CIMDs?
issues.author: josephdecock
issues.body: In section 6.5, there's an exception that allows SSRF checks for the CIMD URL to be skipped when the AS is running on a loopback address. The same exception isn't included in the language about SSRF checks for URLs within the CIMD. Should the same exception apply there?

comments.author: ThisIsMissEm
comments.body:
Feel free to open a pull request to add this. I think it may make sense? I'm not 100% sure. The reason to be careful of SSRF in the property URLs is that a domain name could resolve to loopback.

If both AS and client are on loopback and not publicly exposed to the internet, then yeah, you can probably ignore SSRF checks for those URLs, since you'd be operating in a controlled environment, I think?

issues.title: Ambiguity around `application_type` when metadata defaults and usage patterns point to different client types
issues.author: btiernay
issues.body:
## Summary

The CIMD specification does not appear to say how `application_type` should be handled when it is omitted and therefore defaults to `"web"` under [RFC 7591](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7591), but the client uses redirect URI patterns described in [RFC 8252](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8252) for native applications.

This seems relevant because RFC 8252 also says that redirect URI alone is not enough to distinguish client type, and that the client type needs to be recorded during registration so the authorization server can determine how to treat the client.

That leaves some uncertainty around how CIMD is expected to interact with existing OAuth guidance on client type.

## Problem Description

CIMD reuses the OAuth Dynamic Client Registration metadata model from [RFC 7591](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7591). In that model, `application_type` is client metadata, and if it is omitted, the default value is `"web"`.

At the same time, [RFC 8252](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8252) describes redirect URI patterns used by native applications, including loopback redirect URIs. It also says that redirect URI alone is not sufficient to distinguish a public native app from a confidential web client, and that the client type is required to be recorded during client registration.

This creates a question when:

- `application_type` is omitted, so the RFC 7591 default is `"web"`, and
- the client uses loopback redirect URIs associated with native apps in RFC 8252

## Example

Consider a client that:

- omits `application_type`
- uses loopback redirect URIs such as `http://127.0.0.1` or `http://localhost`

Relevant specifications:

- [RFC 7591](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7591)  
  If `application_type` is omitted, the default is `"web"`.

- [RFC 8252](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8252)  
  Loopback redirect URIs are described for native applications, but redirect URI alone is not enough to determine client type, and the client type is required to be recorded during registration.

In that situation, it is not clear how CIMD expects an authorization server to treat the omitted `application_type`:

| Possible reading | Possible result |
| --- | --- |
| Apply the RFC 7591 default strictly | The recorded client type is `"web"` |
| Treat the configuration as incomplete for RFC 8252 purposes | The client metadata is insufficient until client type is explicitly recorded |

CIMD does not currently seem to say how this case should be understood.

## Interoperability Impact

If different implementations handle this differently, the same client configuration may be accepted in one environment and rejected in another.

This appears to have practical relevance in deployed systems, for example:

- [anthropics/claude-code#18251](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/18251)
- [anthropics/claude-code#37747](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/37747)

Possible effects include:

- inconsistent handling of clients that omit `application_type`
- uncertainty about whether the RFC 7591 default is sufficient in RFC 8252 scenarios
- reduced interoperability across implementations

## Question

When `application_type` is omitted and therefore defaults to `"web"` under RFC 7591, but the client uses loopback redirect URIs described in RFC 8252, is that default intended to be sufficient for recording client type, or is explicit client type metadata expected?

## Motivation

Clarifying this would make it easier to understand how CIMD relates to RFC 8252’s requirement that client type be recorded during registration, especially in cases where `application_type` is omitted and the RFC 7591 default may not reflect the intended client type.

comments.author: ThisIsMissEm
comments.body: We could make `application_type` required in this specification, but we couldn't change how it's inferred as a default value — that'd probably need to be brought up with RFC 7591 / RFC 8252.

comments.author: btiernay
comments.body: @aaronpk would be great to get your input here as well. Personally, I think requiring the application_type seems like the most natural solution to fit within the constraints of the depended-on specs.

comments.author: aaronpk
comments.body:
Turns out `application_type` is not defined in RFC7591, it is defined in OpenID Connect's version of Dynamic Client Registration:

https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-registration-1_0.html#ClientMetadata

RFC7591 doesn't say anything about `application_type` at all.

OAuth 2.1 talks about client types "public" and "confidential", as well as client profiles "web", "browser" and "native", however does not provide any formal definition of client registration parameters for these.

https://drafts.oauth.net/oauth-v2-1/draft-ietf-oauth-v2-1.html#section-2.1

I'm not a huge fan of adding a dependency on OIDC for this.

comments.author: btiernay
comments.body:
You are right that application_type is not defined by RFC 7591 itself. It comes from the OpenID Connect registration spec not core OAuth DCR.

But CIMD Section 4.1 points at the DCR metadata registry and that registry includes entries added by specs beyond RFC 7591. 

https://www.iana.org/assignments/oauth-parameters/oauth-parameters.xhtml#client-metadata

> The client metadata document is a JSON ([RFC8259]) document containing the metadata of the client. The client metadata values are the values defined in the OAuth Dynamic Client Registration Metadata OAuth Parameters registry ...

So the question seems to be less "is application_type in RFC 7591" and more "does CIMD intend to inherit registry entries like application_type with their original semantics even when they originate in OIDC?"

If the answer is yes then the interoperability question still exists, just with a different citation chain. If the answer is no, then CIMD probably needs a clearer OAuth-native way to express the same client-profile information.

comments.author: yissellokta
comments.body:
Hi @aaronpk. The main issue that we have is by default application_type value is `web`. Based on different sections from different RFCs, it hints that for web we should use the https schema, and native is the only one using loopback interface redirect uris. As @btiernay  mentioned, it is a conflict when we try to register redirect uris for CIMD that are using loopback interfaces but the application_type is not provided. Providing a little bit more clarity in the standards will help with this conflict. 

- By OpenID Connect Dynamic Client Registration 1.0, [section 2](https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-registration-1_0-21.html#ClientMetadata)
> Authorization Servers MAY reject Redirection URI values using the http scheme, other than the loopback case for Native Clients. The Authorization Server MUST verify that all the registered redirect_uris conform to these constraints.

- By RFC 8252 (OAuth 2.0 for Native Apps), section [7.3](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8252#section-7.3)
> The authorization server MUST allow any port to be specified at the time of the authorization request for loopback IP redirect URIs

- By RFC 6749 (OAuth 2.0), section [3.1.2.1](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6749#section-3.1.2.1)
> The redirection endpoint SHOULD require the use of TLS as described in [Section 1.6](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6749#section-1.6) when the requested response type is "code" or "token",  or when the redirection request will result in the transmission of sensitive credentials over an open network.

- By OAuth 2.1 (draft-ietf-oauth-v2-1-15), section [8.4.2](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-oauth-v2-1/)
> Loopback interface redirection is a method of receiving the authorization response in a native application

- By OAuth 2.1 (draft-ietf-oauth-v2-1-15), section [1.5](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-oauth-v2-1/)
> All the OAuth protocol URLs (URLs exposed by the AS, RS and Client) MUST use the https scheme except for loopback interface redirect URIs

- By OAuth 2.1 (draft-ietf-oauth-v2-1-15) section [2.3.1](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-oauth-v2-1/)
> The redirection endpoint URI MUST use the 'https' scheme, except for loopback interface redirect URIs, which MAY use the 'http' scheme.

- By RFC 8252 (OAuth 2.0 for Native Apps), [section 7.2](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8252#section-7.2)
> As the redirect URI alone is not enough to distinguish public native app clients from confidential web clients, it is REQUIRED in Section 8.4 that the client type be recorded during client registration to enable the server to determine the client type and act accordingly.

comments.author: aaronpk
comments.body:
I'm re-reading the text in RFC8252 and realizing that the app "type" it is talking about there is only the difference between public and confidential clients.

[Section 7.2](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8252#section-7.2)

> As the redirect URI alone is not enough to distinguish public native
> app clients from confidential web clients, it is REQUIRED in
> [Section 8.4](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8252#section-8.4) that the client type be recorded during client
> registration to enable the server to determine the client type and
> act accordingly.

[Section 8.4](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8252#section-8.4) "Registration of Native App Clients"

> Except when using a mechanism like Dynamic Client Registration
> [[RFC7591](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7591)] to provision per-instance secrets, native apps are
> classified as public clients, as defined by [Section 2.1](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8252#section-2.1) of OAuth 2.0
> [[RFC6749](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6749)]; they MUST be registered with the authorization server as
> such.

This doesn't say native apps are registered as a "native app" type, it says they are registered as public clients, unless they have a per-instance secret in which case they would be registered as a confidential client.

I'm not actually sure how OpenID Connect DCR was published in its current state, since it directly contradicts RFC8252:

OIDC DCR - [Section 2 Client Metadata](https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-registration-1_0.html#ClientMetadata)

> Native Clients MUST only register redirect_uris using custom URI schemes or loopback URLs using the http scheme

That contradicts RFC8252 (and by extension OAuth 2.1) which discusses using [claimed `https` URIs](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8252#section-7.2).

Aside from the `application_type` property itself, what is the actual question here? Is it about whether an AS should allow `localhost`/`127.0.0.1` redirect URIs for a given client?

Things are going to get a bit fuzzier once [Attestation-Based Client Authentication](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-oauth-attestation-based-client-auth/) is more widely adopted too, it's not necessarily going to always be the case that native apps will not have a `jwks_uri` in the client metadata.

comments.author: yissellokta
comments.body:
Yes, all loopback interface redirect uris refer to native applications, in the OAuth 2.0 for Native applications or the OAuth 2.1 draft there is a section for it for native applications. But when we are registering a client we don't know what type of client it is, we only know it is confidential or public, but there is not verification we can make to know what clients we could allow or not to have loopback interface redirect uris. We were using application_type in order to have this mapping with a default value of web, but it conflicts with what we have seen in the CIMD clients. 
What is your recommendation in general, allow any client to use loopback interface redirect_uris, any security concern here?

issues.title: Mixup attacks with issuer identifiers
issues.author: erincandescent
issues.body:
Picture this scenario:

* `example.com` run an AS which implements this spec
* `example.net` also run an AS with issuer identifier `https://example.net/`. 
* `example.com`'s AS is also connected to `example.net` as a client 

If an attacker can place a JSON file at `https://example.net/`, they can use it as a client ID. They can then use the client credentials grant and potentially (depending upon which OAuth 2 extensions are implemented by `example.com`) will mint JWTs with `{"iss":"https://example.com","sub":"https://example.net"}`.They may be able to trigger flows which result in minting OpenID Connect ID tokens `{"iss":"https://example.com","aud":"https://example.net","sub":"usersub"}`. These ID tokens may then be abusable with e.g. the JWT bearer authorization grant, e.g. via the identity chaining spec. 

It may seem unlikely that an attacker can place a JSON file at the root of `https://example.net/`, but consider a setup where a frontend proxy has the configuration:

* `https://example.net/.well-known/` -> forwarded to IDP. The other endpoints of the IDP might live on e.g. `https://auth.example.com/`
* `https://example.net/` -> forwarded to a Wordpress installation with insecure plugins

This attack can be relatively silently executed. The attacker can modify the code at `example.net` to sniff if the request is coming from a web browser (inferable because the user agent starts with `Mozilla`, or because `text/html` is in the accept header, or similar) and return the usual home page, only returning the client ID metadata to clients that look like an AS.

There are three potential ways I see of avoiding this:

1. Embed the client metadata inside the AS metadata document. This makes the `oauth-authorization-server` name misleading (though `openid-connect` less so), but it absolutely guarantees the two entities with the same URI are actually the same entity (In retrospect it might have been better for it to be `/.well-known/oauth` with properties under e.g. `"as"`, `"rp"` and `"rs"`, but that ship sailed a long time ago)
2. Place the client metadata in `/.well-known/oauth-client` and trust that `.well-known` is well controlled. This is probably OK. There may be a hazard here between OAuth and OpenID style uses of `.well-known`...
3. Proceed as is, write several paragraphs of guidance in the Security Considerations section cautioning people about potential for confusion between client IDs and AS issuer IDs and watch as everyone gets it wrong and we create a new rich mine of security vulnerabilities

comments.author: erincandescent
comments.body: This is in some ways a continuation of #38 but this time there are actual security consdierations :/

comments.author: ThisIsMissEm
comments.body: CIMDs don't use `/.well-known/` presently, but any path they want to

comments.author: erincandescent
comments.body: But that's exactly the problem, the client metadata document at `https://example.com/` and the AS configured at `https://example.com/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server` produce the same subject identifier claim values despite being declared in different security domains

comments.author: ThisIsMissEm
comments.body: I can't recall, but we may require a path component that's at least one non-/ character; there was an issue tracking this, I can't recall were we got to with it; cc @aaronpk

comments.author: erincandescent
comments.body:
I don't think requiring a path component really helps; you just have the same problem with issuer identifiers which contain paths (`https://example.com/foo` -> `https://example.com/.well-known/oauth-authorization-server/foo` or `https://example.com/foo/.well-known/openid-configuration`)

In fact I'm not even sure option (2) is secure in the face of both styles of `.well-known` usage existing, but maybe(?) we can reasonably assume that OpenID Connect deployers have secured `.well-known` under the root of their domain...

comments.author: aaronpk
comments.body:
Fun fact: OpenID Connect breaks the premise of `.well-known` by requiring _appending_ the `.well-known/openid-configuration` string to the issuer URL: 

https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-discovery-1_0.html#ProviderConfigurationRequest

```
GET /issuer1/.well-known/openid-configuration
```

comments.author: ThisIsMissEm
comments.body: @erincandescent what's the use case for using a CIMD when you have a known relationship with the AS? `example.com` should be using a direct relationship with `example.net` instead of a CIMD, no?

comments.author: erincandescent
comments.body:
There's a simple answer as to why an AS might want to use both: pushing configuration updates to every "upstream" AS is more work than letting them pull said configuration. This is also what OpenID federation is all about

But in the case in the example the AS was never intentionally using this spec

pulls.title: Suggested improvements
pulls.author: ThisIsMissEm
pulls.body:
This is based on #1, but otherwise has a few language and spelling changes; Additionally:
- added the section about the restriction on the `client_id` property strictly matching the client metadata document's URI
- added recommendation to use a stable URL (I'm not sure if there's a spec to refer to, but it'd be bad practice to, for example, include a version number in the URL for cache busting purposes even though the contents of the document has not actually changed.
- added TBD about possible verification endpoint (i.e., giving developers an easy way to verify their document is valid (or perhaps a mechanism to invalidate a previously cached document?)
- added language to allow extensions to the client metadata document, and allowing it to be served as content types other than just `application/json`, e.g., `application/ld+json`
- moved caching up to beneath errors, as to allow language to easily flow about requirements not to cache error responses or invalid documents.
- added TBD question on the redirect URI matching and whether it is exact string matching or simple string comparison (the original language).

pulls.title: Improve documentation for development usage, prohibiting query parameters in document URLs
pulls.author: ThisIsMissEm
pulls.body:
A pattern that we've seen sometimes used is to have a service generate Client ID Metadata Documents by taking in data from query parameters and transposing those into the contents of the document, this should probably be considered an anti-pattern, since it circumvents a fair few of the security properties of stable URLs for client id metadata documents.

This is noted in #23 and #12, where workarounds have tried to be developed.

For instance, for AtProto / Bluesky, they could host a `client_id.atproto.dev` service which allows you to preregistered a client for development purposes (specifying redirect_uris, scopes, etc), giving back a https URL to a document that you can use for that client.

We could expand this to allow a Authorization Server to apply additional constrains on the contents of the client metadata document, based on the hostname of the document itself, e.g., only documents on `client_id.atproto.dev` hostname can include redirect URIs that are localhost URIs.

pulls.title: Add security consideration for logo_uri usage
pulls.author: ThisIsMissEm
pulls.body:
I'm not 100% happy with the wording here, but this does begin to address the concerns around displaying logos to end-users that are provided from within the client metadata document.

The idea is to provide a security consideration similar to the consideration in Dynamic Client Registration: https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc7591.html#section-5

> In a situation where the authorization server is supporting open client registration, it must be extremely careful with any URL provided by the client that will be displayed to the user (e.g., "logo_uri", "tos_uri", "client_uri", and "policy_uri").  For instance, a rogue client could specify a registration request with a reference to a drive-by download in the "policy_uri", enticing the user to click on it during the authorization.  The authorization server SHOULD check to see if the "logo_uri", "tos_uri", "client_uri", and "policy_uri" have the same host and scheme as the those defined in the array of "redirect_uris" and that all of these URIs resolve to valid web pages.  Since these URI values that are intended to be displayed to the user at the authorization page, the authorization server SHOULD protect the user from malicious content hosted at the URLs where possible.  For instance, before presenting the URLs to the user at the authorization page, the authorization server could download the content hosted at the URLs, check the content against a malware scanner and blacklist filter, determine whether or not there is mixed secure and non-secure content at the URL, and other possible server-side mitigations.  Note that the content in these URLs can change at any time and the authorization server cannot provide complete confidence in the safety of the URLs, but these practices could help.  To further mitigate this kind of threat, the authorization server can also warn the user that the URL links have been provided by a third party, should be treated with caution, and are not hosted by the authorization server itself.  For instance, instead of providing the links directly in an HTML anchor, the authorization server can direct the user to an interstitial warning page before allowing the user to continue to the target URL

Perhaps the section should be also including `tos_uri`, `policy_uri` and `client_uri` (e.g., asserting that the Authorization Server can choose to validate the documents at these URIs and also choose whether or not to display these URIs to the end-user.

comments.author: trwnh
comments.body:
@aaronpk wrote:

> I am a bit rusty on media types but isn't there a way to say `application/cimd+ld+json` or something as a more specific media type than `application/ld+json` that is also compatible with `application/cimd+json`?

Relevant history:

- https://github.com/w3c/did/issues/208
- https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-mediaman-suffixes/01/
- https://github.com/w3c/did/issues/863
- https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-ietf-mediaman-6838bis/

In summary: DID work reached out to IETF about +ld+json as a potential structured suffix, and got a skeptical response in 2020. An RFC draft was filed to update the structured suffix registry in 2022, but after IETF review it was rejected and the RFC draft expired. There is now another draft RFC updating the structured suffix registry to disallow multiple suffixes:

> While it might be desirable to indicate multiple use cases simultaneously using a compound suffix (e.g., "+xml+zip"), experience shows that suffixes are a poor basis for this; the combinations of suffixes quickly multiply, and there is not a well-specified processing model that can handle them safely.  Therefore, multiple suffixes are disallowed from use.

It is possible for the DID work to have pursued something like `application/did+ld` or `application/did+jsonld`, but they ended up going with `application/did` instead. This pattern was adopted for Verifiable Credentials (VC) and Controlled Identifiers (CID) work as well, with `application/vc`, `application/vp`,  and `application/cid`.

## Multiple media types

Unfortunately, this creates a bit of a new quagmire: how do you signal or know when multiple media types apply? https://www.w3.org/TR/vc-data-model-2.0/#media-type-precision describes the concept of "media type precision": 

- application/vc and application/vp are the most specific types, indicating that Verifiable Credentials or Verifiable Presentations semantics and processing are in effect.
- application/ld+json is a less precise media type, indicating a Linked Data processing mode, with explicit semantics provided by the `@context`.
- application/json is even less precise, indicating JSON document processing and no in-band semantics. (Semantics are loaded out-of-band in the application layer.)
- text/plain is even less precise, indicating a plain-text processing mode that renders the content of the document verbatim as a UTF-8 sequence (or some other encoding).
- application/octet-stream is the least precise media type, indicating a processing mode where raw bytes are streamed in sequence.

So we can see that the best practice right now is to be as precise as possible, wherever feasible.

### JSON-LD and Solid-OIDC compatibility

Side note regarding JSON-LD: content type sniffing is also possible in some cases, such as detecting the presence of a `@context` key. However, the JSON-LD context may be missing, in which case applications using JSON-LD processing can make use of "context injection" to kinda convert the more specific types to `application/ld+json`, using out-of-band application knowledge/semantics to recognize which media types can be converted like this (and which contexts to inject): https://www.w3.org/TR/cid-1.0/#context-injection

Solid-OIDC specifically does not require any specific Accept type; it currently requires `application/ld+json` for the *response* only, and also makes an exception for content negotiation: https://solidproject.org/TR/oidc#clientids-document

> When a Client Identifier is dereferenced, the resource MUST be serialized as an application/ld+json document unless content negotiation requires a different outcome.

It seems like CIMD doesn't require the *response* to use `Content-Type: application/cimd+json`, which means for compatibility, the CIMD processor should probably be prepared to accept less precise media types such as `application/ld+json` or `application+json` 

## Summary

- Clients can always request the most specific type of `application/cimd+json`, to indicate that they are CIMD processors and intend to process the returned content as a CIMD document serialized in JSON.
- Servers should be prepared to respond to requests for `application/cimd+json`.
  - If the server supports negotiating for `application/cimd+json`, it can return 200 OK with the CIMD.
  - If the server has a default representation, it can return the CIMD as the default representation.
  - Edge case: the server might return 300 Multiple Choices for client-driven content negotiation. This should be recommended against.
  - Edge case: the server might return 406 Not Acceptable, if it strictly recognizes only certain content types as having representations. This should also be recommended against.
- Clients should be prepared to receive a less precise Content-Type in the response, and should not treat this as an error.
  - Potential security consideration for "raw" servers which accept user-generated content. These servers may use `Content-Type: text/plain` or `Content-Type: application/octet-stream` as an indication that the content should not be processed with more specific processing models. (I am not sure if this allows for CIMD-based impersonation, or if there are other security considerations that prevent this from being an issue.)

### Recommended language to add

- Something about serving CIMD documents as the default representation:
  > "The client metadata document MUST be the default representation when content negotiation is used. The HTTP server hosting the client metadata document MUST NOT use client-driven content negotiation (responding with HTTP 300 Multiple Choices). The HTTP server hosting the client metadata document also MUST NOT respond with 406 Not Acceptable."
- Something about clients not erroring out on other Content-Type values in the response:
  > "The HTTP server hosting the client metadata document might be configured to respond with a Content-Type other than application/cimd+json, so the Authorization Server MUST be prepared to process valid client metadata documents served with less precise media types. These less precise media types MAY include application/json or application/ld+json."
  - ...maybe briefly include a note about media type precision?
  - describe in more detail exactly when the document "conforms to `application/<AS-defined>+json`" 
    - btw you left a "`application/<AS-defined>+json`" in there, so replace that placeholder :)
  - is any +json suffix okay here?

comments.author: ThisIsMissEm
comments.body:
I think on:
> Clients should be prepared to receive a less precise Content-Type in the response, and should not treat this as an error. 

We could use language that says:
> Authorization Servers requesting Client ID Metadata Documents SHOULD expect to receive a less specific `Content-Type` in response to the requested `Accept: application/cimd+json`, where the server MAY respond with either `application/cimd+json` or `application/json`

Would that make sense? I'm also saying "Authorization Servers" there instead of "Clients" because the OAuth Relying Party "client application" would generally not actually need to "request" the CIMD, though, of course anything can request a CIMD. I just want to set the expectations for an Authorization Server more explicitly.

That language also precludes the usage of `text/plain` and `application/octet-stream`, which tbh, I'd treat those responses as a potential security error.

comments.body:
@ThisIsMissEm wrote:

> edge cases of returning 300 / 406 by requiring the response status code of 200

the language could be more clear; "require 200" implies "default representation" to avoid the 300 or 406.

---

@ThisIsMissEm wrote:

> We could use language that says:

i don't think the requester of a CIMD "SHOULD expect to receive a less specific Content-Type", but rather they "SHOULD be prepared to handle responses with a less specific Content-Type". it wouldn't be weird for the CIMD host to serve `application/cimd+json` if it's already capable of `Accept`ing that type.

@ThisIsMissEm wrote:

> precludes the usage of `text/plain` and `application/octet-stream`, which tbh, I'd treat those responses as a potential security error

the earlier language already precludes this sort of: "These less precise media types MAY include application/json or application/ld+json"

what i left out was an explicit requirement that the CIMD requester SHOULD NOT / MUST NOT proceed with text/plain or application/octet-stream, though. i also left out any considerations of +json as a structured suffix. i'm not sure that these kinds of responses are always or generally security errors; it depends on whether the CIMD requester can be potentially misled by a text file which happens to parse as a valid CIMD, and i don't see how...

pulls.title: Add `software_version` to identify metadata changes
pulls.author: itsvs
pulls.body:
The -01 draft suggests that clients should refrain from updating metadata too frequently, and that authorization servers should expect occasional changes to metadata. It might be helpful for clients to make it easy for servers to identify when metadata has changed.

RFC 7591 provides a `software_version` field that we could use for this purpose. This PR introduces a couple of recommendations along these lines:
- Clients may include this field to indicate when the metadata document has changed and/or to differentiate between application versions with different capabilities
- If clients parameterize this in the URL, authorization servers could treat different versions as different clients with different policies.

I briefly chatted about this with Aaron before making this PR, and I am very much open to discussing further!

comments.author: aaronpk
comments.body: Vaguely related to #57 as well

comments.author: ThisIsMissEm
comments.body: In the wild these two different URLs would be treated as two distinct clients, with no carry over between them. Is that what you're intending here?

comments.author: itsvs
comments.body:
Yes. Authorization servers may wish to enforce different policies based on client capabilities, so if those vary across client versions, the versions should be treated as different clients. Updating the same client wouldn't work since multiple versions of a client might exist at any given time (e.g. if users need to update the client app).

That being said, I don't think clients have to host _every_ new software version at a separate URL. If it's something like a logo change, the client might just update `software_version` to `2.0.1` and leave the URL as `2.0`. URL changes should really be reserved for things that would make a meaningful difference to an AS (but this is left up to the client implementer's judgment).

comments.author: itsvs
comments.body:
As mentioned in #55, I want to dial back the answer above. I said:

> the versions should be treated as different clients

But after following the trust boundary thread, I think this is more of a "may" than a "should." The different versions can link to each other to suggest to the AS that they are the same, and _then_ it remains up to the AS to decide whether to treat them as the same client or different ones.

pulls.title: Clarify loopback exception for SSRF checks
pulls.author: josephdecock
pulls.body:
This clarifies that SSRF checks for both the CIMD itself and URLs within it should be handled the same way: don't fetch special use IPs unless everything is running on loopback. 

This change does normatively strengthen the requirements for URLs within the CIMD. My assumption is that the current wording of MUST NOT for the CIMD itself with an exception for running on loopback and SHOULD NOT for contained URLs is meant to accomplish the same thing, and I thought combining the two made it read more clearly.

See also #73.
